sentences.text: I know the-- it was a horrible time for us in the ghetto, because already-- and I was always complaining to my-- Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH]-- that it's impossible to live in such bad-- I don't know.
Conditions-- that I will be able-- not be able to fulfill my work.
I was saying this in German, actually.
I always-- when I have a word with him-- what has to be done, what was done, and this all-- so I told him, [GERMAN].
And I have been many, many times telling him about the conditions in which we are living and that I would not be able to really fulfill my work.
From the other side, I was frightened also, because working, you know, for the frightening, that-- that for-- for saboteurs, as-- that something could happen, you know?
And I will not be able really to fill out the work for them.
So he was [NON-ENGLISH].
Really, many, many times he was quite an [NON-ENGLISH].
They are doing every possible thing to take us out from the ghetto.
Who-- who do you mean by us?
The people, what they were working for the tanneries-- the Jews.
And the Jews was waiting for the [NON-ENGLISH] no-- [NON-ENGLISH].
And that happened that it's quite possible the Gauleiter was Murer at that time.
Gauleiter-- that is the head of the-- of Lithuania, of like a governor-- a governor-- a SR man, of course.
Murer.
M-U-R-E-R.
And they were most probably-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --giving him a lot of presents-- [LAUGHS] --maybe.
And he gave the OK of taking out the [NON-ENGLISH] from the ghetto.
And that was built.
That was in-- in-- sorry-- in December '41.
And that was built.
Kailis, they called this, the building-- two blocks of flats.
And 1,000-- about 1,500 people went out, because the-- the workers with the wives and the-- and the families out from ghetto and separate.
They established two blocks on the-- on the name, Kailis.
K-A-I-L-I-E-I-S-- Kailis.
From ghetto one, that was quite-- that was on the periphery-- how to call it?
The periphery of the town, Vilna--
--not far from the factory where the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the [NON-ENGLISH] had been working, a little bit further from the tanneries.
And we were also going in [NON-ENGLISH].
That's mean in a group-- in groups-- to work, and from work, coming back.
It was--
--oh, it was-- it was a lot better, because every family got a room for themself.
There were three room or four room-- three, I believe-- a room-- flats, you know, with the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --entrance-- a big entrance, a bathroom, a kitchen, and so on.
So all of this in every flat.
That was three families living.
They were two blocks-- block number one, and block number two there.
I was in block number one.
And I had the room that was on the second floor-- on the second floor--
--the first room against the entrance of that flat.
In the beginning, that was only by police in-- Jewish police what was formed.
But that took only nearly a year that the SS made from that arbeiter block, [NON-ENGLISH] They make concentrations arbeiter block number one and number two.
And, of course, that was a way to-- what they are calling the guards on--
--on towers, yes, on the four-- on the four corners of that block.
And they were-- and they went by the entrance, a guard, a SS man that the times were the worst, that time.
But still, we got the contact with the outside world by going to work and-- with working with Poles and so on.
The beginning was better, because we were more freed.
We could bringing-- buying-- [NON-ENGLISH]--
Provisions.
And it was better than in ghettos.
There is no comparison with the-- with the-- the conditions that are living in ghetto.
It was impossible.
And when the SS took over, it start to get worst, of course.
And selections starts to be-- the action for children till 13 years.
Because people were still with the families there with children.
The first bad action-- selection-- not selection-- it's for children till 13 years.
So the all children were taken away-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--and gassed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 194-- '42-- yes.
I can't recollect time-- the time exactly when they--
Kailis, yes.
That was the worst action that I can describe-- children-- beautiful children, lovely children-- till 13 years-- till 13 years age.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I beg you?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] For us, what left over for the parents, for the mothers, for the fathers, it's not describable!
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
Just--
I can't follow you that properly.
Yes.
Oh, at the [NON-ENGLISH], in the-- in the-- in the tanneries--
--amongst the tanners, yeah.
Till I escaped to the partisans.
Exactly-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Yes.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I helped, financially, even when I was in ghetto, the time which I was in ghetto, till establishing the Kailis block.
I was helping people there.
There are two families.
And I got the written statements, a written-- a lot of material what was written about me, what I was doing.
Financially-- financially with money.
The-- get?
I will tell you.
The-- during the Lithuanians, which taking over the high ranking positions that combine the Lithuanians, were stealing a lot of goods and selling them at the market.
I don't know what they were doing later on.
And to shut my mouth, they were throwing to me dollars-- not to open my mouth and not to see nothing.
Amongst them, there was one on the name, Pikoris, Pikoris-- P-I-K-O-R-I-S.
The other one was Butisas-- B-U-T-I-S-A-S.
The others, I don't remember all the number-- the-- the names.
Especially that Pikoris, always, when he was bribing me, so to say, he was throwing in me packs of dollars.
And the money I used to help other Jews.
Hiding it-- not in the ghetto.
I was hiding the money actually in the factories.
And one might be there still existing.
It would tell a lot of stories-- that.
That what I am telling you is only just a-- just a drop.
And whenever I need some money to help or to help for myself, I was getting help from that hiding, where the money was lying.
That was amongst-- that was in one of the big drier rooms there.
And some of the bricks were taken out.
That was a little-- where I was hiding that-- everything there.
Being in ghetto, I was helping.
You asked me who-- if I helped.
That was one-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
S-W-I-R-S-K-E I-- sorry-- I-- for Swirski.
And the story, I would do this in short.
There were three brothers, three of four brothers.
I can't tell you exactly.
I believe there are three-- there are the three brothers.
And they immigrated to Israel.

And he was the one that a few months before the-- the war broke out between Russia and Germany, he came to Poland to liquidate some of sawmills-- to liquidate, to sell it, and-- and the war broke out.
And he went out-- he, his wife, and his son-- and a one son.
Once I was in the ghetto and just going with that [NON-ENGLISH]-- with another chap with the name Edelman.
And he mentioned to me, showing me on a person, that he's sitting in-- next to a entrance in one of the houses there, and selling some timber, when you are starting a fire.
I don't know how you call.
Kindling.
Yes.
So I said to him, if you would know who that chap is and what he's doing now, you will-- if-- if you could help him, please, Kaufman, help him.
So he went to him.
He said, listen.
That gentleman want to help you.
So I said to him, what kind he-- he wants to help.
He wants to give you some money to help you.
So he was-- no, no.
No.
He's only-- he's only giving here as a loan.
Don't-- don't-- don't-- don't worry.
Don't worry.
Don't worry.
So he accept this.
And I got the written things about this, that I took out $500 and I give him $500.
The other one was Alperowitz.
A-L-P-E-R-O-W-I-T-Z-- most probably.
Yeah.
That was he and his wife.
That was in the ghetto, when I-- so long as I was in ghetto before I went to Kailis.
And the same thing, one of my friends asked me if that is possible that he knows him and so on.
He was from Kaunas, from Kovno, from Lithuania-- that he was a manufacturer, a owner of a-- a manufacturer from webbing things and so on.
And he's in such a bad-- that he can't go to Kaunas.
He not-- he is in Vilna.
He's now in the ghetto and has to-- if it's possible if I could help him.
I helped him too.
And I got those from him.
Letters are written about the help I gave him.
That was the time when I was in ghetto.
And Kailis-- I helped a lot of people too.
And amongst others, that is the main help what I gave is to the director of the Yeshiva, of the Vilna Yeshiva, Rabbi Lvowicz.
He was with his family and a few people from the Yeshiva.
That was in Kailis already.
Lvowicz-- L-V-O-W-I-C-Z.
From time to time, I was helping him out always with big amounts of money-- thousands of dollars.
And I got-- actually, I left the whole original papers I wrote.
I got, because I hide them by one of the Poles, one of my friends-- Poles in-- in Vilna.
And after the war, I got this out-- everything out.
And he-- really, I helped him a tremendous lot-- a tremendous lot.
He was arrived in Kailis different time-- different-- whenever-- when I was giving him a sum of money, 1,000 of the-- 2,000, or something like that.
So he was always going on the attic and writing letters to his in-laws.
One of them was the grand rabbi, [NON-ENGLISH] in Johannesburg.
Letters are written to his brothers-- a rabbi in Brooklyn, a rabbi in Brooklyn-- written-- written to his family a lot--
--telling them what is going on with him and what I was done to him.
Always, letters are written when I was advancing him with some money.
The money, when you are asking me where from, they came, they come from-- from that to shut me up, my mouth-- from the money what I was getting from them, from the Pikoris.
No, no, no.
That-- that-- what I was doing, that I brought this out from my home, from my father, to help people, not to live for yourself, but to think about others too.
Lvowicz.
No.
It was no mail list.
It was only letters.
He had his own-- he was himself.
He didn't know what will be, and just telling who was-- what I was doing to him and how he could leave, that he could leave in such bad times, that he could leave.
So these-- these notes weren't sent anywhere, though.
They were kept as a record of the-- of the--
A record what I was doing, actually-- writing about-- mostly, mostly writing about me, what I was doing.
We were getting news from people that they escape.
They came, and they tell us what is going on there-- especially to contract what I've got, what I got more with my father, the correspondence what went through Poles.
Because Jews, being in ghetto in Radom, couldn't write.
It that correct?
The same thing applies to me.
So that was a family in-- in Radom, Bolesta-- Bolesta.
Through them, letters were written to me, to a family in Vilna.
But I can't recollect the-- the name of the-- the family in Vilna.
Could you spell the Bolesta name, please?
B-O-L-E-S-T-A.
And because my father bring-- that was-- that wasn't in-- in the time when the war broke out already between-- that was before.
I'm talking-- sorry.
I'm talking about-- it's mixed up a little bit.
I'm talking about the time when I was working for the Russian army, Red Army, and before for [NON-ENGLISH] in the Omega, straightaway at the beginning of the war.
So I got the letters.
And my father asked me to help him, to help them, to help them-- the daughter of Dr-- of the director, of Jack, that what I mentioned to you already in the-- previously.
So you had no-- are you saying you had no contact--
After I haven't got--
--no contact whatsoever.
Unfortunately, I have to mention here things what are not-- it's hard for me to talk about this.
But still, I have to tell it.

When Vilna start judenrein, become judenrein, and the ghetto was to the concentration, to the extermination camps-- sent away, a day before it happens, that that Rabbi Lvowicz with the wife-- because they didn't have already the children-- they were taken away-- two children-- a little boy, Nomi, and the other one, I forget the name.
One of the commandants from our bock took that Rabbi Lvowicz with the wife and send them to the ghetto, for sending him away to the examination camps.
The name of that commandant from the police, from the police there, was Krawczynskae.
K-R-A-W-C-Z-Y-N-S-K-A-E-- Lolek.
His first name was Lolek.
He came from my work, from the tanneries back.
I find out what happens.
I ask him what happens.
He said, you want to have your rabbi back here?
The test will cost-- will cost you $300.
I told him, Lolek, I accept this.
Bring me back the rabbi.
You will get the $300.
Was Lolek with the Jewish police?
That was the Jewish police.
He commanded the-- the Jewish police.
They are different.
They were different.
I don't blame the whole police.
Because people that they were working with us in the underground-- they were working with us in-- by the partisan.
They have been by the partisan-- commandants.
But they have been lower persons like him leaving the-- no-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I gave him the money.
The rabbi?
No.
The money was given to him.
When I told him that he is getting the $300, he was sure that he will get the $300.
He brought back the rabbi.
And a 1/2 an hour, the rabbi was back with the wife-- was back.
Good.
